stdout:
I’ve confirmed there’s a new `HEAD` after the last failed review. Next I’m validating the repo and checking only the latest follow-up changes plus any remaining unresolved blockers.

The core validation is still running in the background. While that finishes, I’m pulling the exact changed files and the design/doc/test surfaces tied to the last failed review so I can verify what was actually fixed.

Core validation finished cleanly. I’m reading the full output now and then I’ll spot-check the doc/API/test changes that were specifically called out in the last failed review.

I’ve confirmed the latest commit only touched four follow-up files, and most earlier blockers are fixed. I’m now checking for the last remaining review risks: validation of the JS/MCP side and whether the preset docs actually match the implemented CLI behavior.

I found one concrete mismatch still worth testing: the docs now promise builtin-preset overrides, but the CLI still appears to reject creating a preset with a builtin name. I’m also rerunning the web/MCP validation via a task agent since the direct shell call was blocked.

I’m down to the final edge checks. The code/tests look much better, but I’m verifying whether there’s any remaining contract mismatch between the preset implementation, the CLI, and the docs before I call the verdict.

I want one clean answer on the JS side before finalizing. I’m trying a direct shell invocation for the remaining web/MCP commands, since the sub-agent hit an environment-level permission issue after `npm test`.

I found a more substantive issue than just wording: overridden builtins are still reported as `[builtin]` in the preset list because origin tracking is lost in `PresetManager`. I’m confirming where that surfaces so the review can cite the real user-facing impact.

## Acceptance criteria

**PASS.** The deferred web-mutation scope is now reflected in the issue description, so the remaining acceptance target is the preset work. The design’s `security-audit` preset requires `security-review`, `secret-detection`, and `dependency-audit` (`dev/active/production-polish-design.md:194-231`), and the implementation now includes all three (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/builtin.rs:257-304`).

## Prior review feedback

**PARTIAL.** The latest follow-up fixed the previously cited missing `secret-detection` gate, replaced the placeholder breakdown tests (`crates/jit/tests/breakdown_tests.rs:166-204,278-295`), and updated the preset docs (`docs/reference/cli-commands.md:1253-1285`).

A new blocking issue remains in the preset-listing path: overridden builtin presets are still reported as builtin.

## Architecture and separation of concerns

**PASS.** The current diff stays within preset definitions, preset loading, tests, and CLI docs. I did not find a new domain/storage/commands/CLI boundary violation in the reviewed range.

## Functional paradigm

**PASS.** The touched Rust code remains in line with repo style; no blocking imperative regression stood out.

## Correctness and invariants

**FAIL.** `PresetManager::list_presets()` marks a preset as builtin solely by checking whether its name appears in `BuiltinPresets::names()` (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:113-123`). That loses source-of-truth once a custom preset overrides a builtin name, even though the loader explicitly allows the custom file to replace the builtin definition in-memory (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:31-35`).

That bad `builtin` flag is surfaced directly to users by `jit gate preset list`, which prints `[builtin]` vs `[custom]` from that field (`crates/jit/src/main.rs:1502-1506`). So an overridden `minimal` preset will still be listed as `[builtin]`, contradicting the documented contract that overriding custom presets appear as custom (`docs/reference/cli-commands.md:1251,1285,1316-1318`).

## Safety

**PASS.** No unsafe code issue found in the reviewed paths.

## Testing

**FAIL.** Core Rust validation is green: `./scripts/jit-validate.sh`, `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`, and `cargo fmt --all -- --check` all passed in this review session. `web`’s `npm test` also passed.

But the new bug above is not covered. The tests verify override lookup (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:234-246`) and non-colliding custom listing (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:266-279`) separately, but there is no test asserting that an overridden builtin is listed as custom. That leaves the user-facing regression undetected.

## Error handling

**PASS.** No new blocking error-handling issue stood out in the current diff.

## CLI contract

**FAIL.** The `--json` shape still exists, but the semantic contract is wrong for preset listing: the exported `builtin` field can be false-to-fact for overridden presets because it is derived from name membership rather than actual source (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:113-123`). That makes machine-readable output inaccurate.

## Documentation

**FAIL.** The docs now say overridden presets are custom and appear with a `[custom]` indicator (`docs/reference/cli-commands.md:1251,1285,1316-1318`), but the implementation still reports overridden builtin names as builtin (`crates/jit/src/gate_presets/manager.rs:113-123`, `crates/jit/src/main.rs:1502-1506`). The documentation is therefore ahead of the actual behavior.

## Jit issue management

**PASS.** The provided dependencies are satisfied (`done` or explicitly `rejected` as appropriate). `./scripts/jit-validate.sh` passed; the remaining label warnings are allowed by the review instructions.

VERDICT: FAIL
---
Gate result: FAILED
